1.label: Question 1
1.scenario: Company A is a health and care insurance company operating in Country X. Country Y is a neighbouring country to Country X with very similar but not identical language and culture as Country X. Company A's management is proposing to enter Country Y's insurance market by setting up an insurance company in Country Y. This insurance company will specialise in selling Critical Illness (CI) products to group schemes sponsored by Country Y's employers. Company A is carrying out preliminary investigations into whether Country Y will be a suitable market to enter.

i.question: Describe the main features of a group CI product.

Indicative Solution: The product pays a benefit if any of the events below occurs: upon the occurrence of an event, independent of its extent. on reaching a defined degree of impairment; or on undergoing a surgical procedure.

The illness or conditions covered by the product are perceived by the public to be serious and to occur frequently, can be clearly defined so that there is no ambiguity at the time of claim and sufficient data is available to price the benefit.
Examples of major conditions covered by the product include cancer, coronary artery by-pass surgery, heart attack, kidney failure, major organ transplant, multiple sclerosis and stroke.
The policy is issued to a group (typically an employer) rather than individuals.
In such a case, the group/employer is the policyholder whist the individuals are the insureds.
The employer may pay all the premiums or require that the employees also pay a portion of the premiums.
There is limited underwriting at the point of inception of the policy.
However, employees have to be "actively at work" for them to be eligible for cover.
A free cover limit may be set above which the individual will be required to undergo medical underwriting.
Employees are usually automatically enrolled in the scheme. This will reduce anti-selection hence the minimal underwriting effort.
Group schemes can benefit from economies of scale, leading to lower premiums compared to individual CI policies.
A conversion benefit may be added to the product, enabling employees to continue coverage even if they leave the employer, though usually at a higher cost.

ii.question: Suggest possible reasons why Company A would want to set up an insurance company in Country Y and specialise in selling group CI products.

Indicative Solution: Profitability: Because Company A expects to write profitable CI business in Country Y which can lead to higher overall profits for Company A assuming a share of the profits from the new insurance company will be repatriated to Company A.
Demand: The company may have observed significant demand for critical illness products in Country Y.
The government may have introduced incentives for insurer to provide critical insurance products in Country Y hence Country A wants to exploit the opportunity.
The highly publicized deaths or illnesses of prominent people from dreadful diseases may have created more demand for critical illness products.
The government may have introduced legislation requiring employers to provide critical illness cover for their employees.
The government may have introduced favourable tax treatment for employers with group CI arrangements for their employees.
Country Y's specific features: Country Y has been experiencing significant economic growth hence Company A wants to also exploit the economic growth in the country.
The similarities between Country X and Country Y reduce barriers to entry, such as translation costs and cultural adaptation of products.
The country may have introduced investment promotion incentives for companies opening in the country thus creating an opportunity for Company A.
The company may actually be following its competitors who have invested in Country Y and achieved superior returns.
Country Y may have lower CI insurance penetration than Country X, presenting a significant growth opportunity.
The earnings and disposable income in Country Y creates a rich potential customer base with greater disposable income and a higher likelihood of affording CI coverage.
Country Y's regulatory regime promotes growth of insurers in the country hence Company A intends to exploit such opportunities.
Country X specific issues: Company A may be diversifying away from Country X in order to reduce concentration risk of operating in one market only.
Country X might be experiencing poor economic conditions which are making it difficult for the company to remain operating in Country X. Hence the company wants to gradually divest out of the market.
The insurance market in Country X may have reached saturation, and the growth of Company A has also plateaued in the process.
Country X may be having too much competition due to a high number of health and care insurers in the market which is harming industry profit margins.
Company A Strategic Considerations: Company A may be planning to expand into various markets as part of its growth strategy.
The company may have significant free assets hence opening in a new market will enable the company to utilise the free assets.

iii.question: Discuss the factors that Company A should consider before deciding whether to proceed with the proposal to set up an insurance company in Country Y.

Indicative Solution: Financial Considerations: Consider the profitability for business written in Country Y relative to business written in Country A.
Consider the required profitability targets for the new insurance company, such as payback period, return on capital employed, NPV.
Consider what sales volumes/business mix is required to reach profitability target.
Consider how sensitive profits are to differences between assumptions and experience.
Consider the capital requirements for setting up an insurance company in Country Y and for business written in Country Y.
Consider how the capital requirements for setting up the new insurance company in Country Y will be funded.
Regulatory environment: Company A must understand the regulatory requirements for one to open insurance operations in Country Y.
The minimum capital requirements may present a barrier to entry if returns on capital will take longer to be within the expectations of the company.
The application process and regulator's response time may also need to be considered as it will determine when it can be feasible to start operating in Country Y.

The Company A may want to second some of its staff to Country Y but the employment laws may not allow foreigners to work in the country which may limit the control that Company A will have on its new operations.
Recruiting a completely new team in Country Y may result in different approaches and culture between the two operations in Country X and Country Y.
The tax laws in country Y especially treatment of foreign owned insurers may significantly reduce returns for the company.
This may be more apparent if the tax laws impose higher tax rates for dividends payable to foreign shareholders.
Company A should also consider whether the exchange control regulations to repatriate profits seamlessly back to Country X.
Inability to repatriate profits may eventually create a dead asset and shareholders may apply a significant discount on the company's stock because of that.
Competition: The company should consider the level of competition in the Group CI market in Country Y.
If no other insurer, is active or has significant market share in the market; Company A can be able to build market share in a shorter period and benefit from first mover advantage.
However, if there are already well-established players, it may be difficult to build its own brand equity and market share which will delay the time profitability and overall returns.

In the same vein, though there may not be a key player in the Group CI market, the Company A should consider whether there are players providing other health and care insurance products who can quickly react and launch products in the group CI space.
The pricing strategies of competition are also important to assess possibility of being engaged in pricing warfare and suffering thin profit margins.
Size of the Market: Entry into Country Y needs to be accompanied by a large market size that can sustain the revenue and profit targets that the company targets for.
Market analysis should therefore be carried out including how many employers, how many workers and the range of wage bills per company so as to determine potential income.
The company may want to follow employers who have operations in both Country X and Country Y especially those who are already clients of Company A in Country X.
A market survey can also help the company to determine the level of potential demand for its products in Country Y.
Distribution Channels: The company should consider the distribution channels that are more prevalent in Country Y.
Distributors in the country may not be willing to be seen to be working with a new foreign company and betraying the current players in the market.
On the other side, introducing its own unique distribution channels has the risk that it will take time for the new channels to sufficiently reach the target market.
The commission structures being used in the market must be analysed to determine if they can be sustainable based on the company's pricing and marketing strategy.
Current distributors may also be consulted on their views about the group CI products and whether they would be willing to also sell the product for the company.
Cultural Nuances: The Country Y has been said to have generally similar culture when compared against X hence Company A may not face challenges.
However, though slight, there may be language barriers if staff is seconded to Country Y from Country X.
Citizens of Country Y may have adverse views on buying insurance products from new foreign owned companies due to patriotism.
Subtle cultural differences between Country X and Country Y could impact product design and marketing messages.
Operational Considerations: Establishing infrastructure in Country Y may be a significant capital expenditure item that can reduce the long-term returns for the company.
This will be more pronounced if public infrastructure and amenities are generally of poor quality in Country Y.
Company A should consider whether the new company can employ sufficient suitably trained staff to run the insurance company in Country Y,
… an associated cost of recruitment.
Company A will need to consider if it will use external service providers for certain key area.

If external service providers are brought in the company will also need to decide whether it can bring the service providers who are currently working with them in Country X or consider local service providers.
Service providers from Country X may not have sufficient understanding of the approval processes e.g. for building renovations in Country Y which can delay completion of setting up infrastructure.
Some of the support functions can still be provided from Country X e.g. the customer call centre can still be virtually accessed from Country X to save costs.

Company A will need to consider if there are any regulatory restrictions on whether certain functions have to be carried out in Country Y, or whether it will allow Company A to provide services from Country A.
Financing: The source of the capital to invest in the new venture will need to be considered relative to the expected returns from the venture.
If the company is using free assets, the cost of capital for the company may not be significant.
The company may have to defer payment of dividends to fund the expansion which may be viewed negatively by shareholders.
Debt capital can also be expensive for the company and may not be consistent with the payback periods of the product.
Other Considerations: There will be a need to get reinsurance support for the products it will launch in Country Y.
Hence the company can consult its current reinsurers and solicit their support for the expansion product.
Stakeholder communication will be important to ensure all stakeholders understand the rationale of the project and are supportive.
Regulators in Country X will require assurance that the company has sufficient resources to expand in Country Y without risking the operations in Country X.

Shareholders will need to understand how the expansion drive will deliver superior returns for the company going into the long term and that no rights issue may be required in the future to finance the project.
Support from the fiscal and monetary authorities may be required to enable the company to transfer capital to Country Y without delays.
There is need to consider the potential impact on Company A's reputation if the expansion into Country Y faces challenges or negative publicity.

iv_a.question:
Company A's preliminary investigations identified the following facts:

• The overall (i.e. all critical illnesses combined) morbidity experience over the last 20 years had been lower in Country Y than Country X.
• Historical inflation rates had been much lower in Country Y than Country X in the last 5 years.
• The average salary in Country Y is marginally higher than Country X.

Discuss how Company A may make use of these facts in the following processes for the group CI products to be sold in Country Y:

product design

Indicative Solution: The company can exploit the low morbidity experience to provide more comprehensive benefits in Country Y compared to Country X.
This may include extending the list of illnesses covered by the product since diseases out of the major conditions may not add significantly to the product cost.
However, whilst morbidity may be lower overall, it may be higher for particular illnesses hence analysis would be required to determine illnesses with higher morbidity rates.
Depending on the results of the analysis, the company may find merit in then differentiating some of the limits accordingly to particular major illnesses.
The insurer may consider adding riders to enhance coverage, such as for cancer relapse or specific conditions more common in Country Y.
Low inflation rates may mean that fixed sum assured products may be acceptable in Country Y since customers won't need to worry about the benefit being eroded by inflation.
However, it's likely that group CI products would already be having benefits based on multiple of salary hence would be responsive to salary inflation.
Higher salaries may indicate both the employer and the employee have higher disposable income hence higher benefit multiples would remain affordable to the customers.
However, care may still be required as significantly higher benefits may result in over-insurance for customers.
Given the low morbidity and inflation rates, the company can consider adding guarantees and options as their cost may not be significantly onerous.
These can include allowing the employees to remain insured after stopping being employed by the company.
The potential for favourable morbidity experience can enable the company to consider adding premium refund benefits to the product.

iv_b.question:
Company A's preliminary investigations identified the following facts:

• The overall (i.e. all critical illnesses combined) morbidity experience over the last 20 years had been lower in Country Y than Country X.
• Historical inflation rates had been much lower in Country Y than Country X in the last 5 years.
• The average salary in Country Y is marginally higher than Country X.

Discuss how Company A may make use of these facts in the following processes for the group CI products to be sold in Country Y:

underwriting

Indicative Solution: Though underwriting of group schemes is already relaxed, the company can consider further relaxation of underwriting due to low morbidity rates being experienced in Country Y.
This can be done through increasing the free cover limits to more than the usual free cover limits that the company uses in Country X.
This is further supported by the higher salaries which mean that if free cover limits aren't changed, more proportion of customers will be required to go for medical underwriting.
This can make the product unmarketable and limit business volumes.
The higher salaries and low inflation cause higher disposable incomes which can increase lifestyle diseases in the long term.
Hence the insurer can still consider lifestyle factors like smoking and obesity in underwriting and offer premium discounts for healthier lifestyles.

Care should also be taken that there are major morbidity differences across occupations hence, the company may still need to assess occupation-based risks within Country Y, as certain professions might have different morbidity profile.

iv_c.question:
Company A's preliminary investigations identified the following facts:

• The overall (i.e. all critical illnesses combined) morbidity experience over the last 20 years had been lower in Country Y than Country X.
• Historical inflation rates had been much lower in Country Y than Country X in the last 5 years.
• The average salary in Country Y is marginally higher than Country X.

Discuss how Company A may make use of these facts in the following processes for the group CI products to be sold in Country Y:

pricing.

Indicative Solution: The company can reflect the lower morbidity experience in Country Y by offering more competitive premiums compared to Country X.
There will be need to benchmark premiums against competitors in Country Y to ensure competitiveness while maintaining profitability.
The company may still need to explore reinsurance options to mitigate the risk of unexpected high claims, even with the lower overall morbidity.
Whilst morbidity rates have been lower than in Country X, the company also needs to assess the general trend of the morbidity rates over the 20 years.
This will enable the company to reflect future morbidity rates accurately especially if the morbidity rates are gradually increasing.
While aiming for competitive pricing, the company should ensure adequate profit margins are built into the premiums to account for unforeseen circumstances.
It also remains important for the company to consider currency exchange rate fluctuations between Country X and Country Y when setting premiums and managing financial risks.

Company A needs to be careful that it does not improve product design, relax underwriting requirements and weaken pricing assumptions all at the same time as this could have a material adverse impact on profitability.
Company A should therefore consider the overall impact of changes to product design, underwriting and pricing together before deciding on what changes to make.
Modelling is potentially required to assess overall product profitability remains at acceptable levels.

2.label: Question 2
2.scenario: Company B is a large health and care insurance company in Country Z. It offers a full range of health and care insurance products and is a market leader in Country Z's Income Protection (IP) insurance market.

i_a.question:
Describe briefly the following product features in the context of IP products:

rehabilitation benefits

Indicative Solution: They are paid to claimants who return to work but in a reduced capacity.
The new work is likely to be a lower paying occupation than the occupation the customer had before the claim.
The revised benefit will be adjusted proportionate to the reduction in gross salaries pre-claim and post-claim.
This is done to incentivise claimants to return to work without paying windfall benefits to the claimants.

Rehabilitation can also apply to the process of counselling, whereby disability counsellors assist disabled persons with advice on practical matters to do with the benefit and their disability, in order to aid a return to work.

i_b.question:
Describe briefly the following product features in the context of IP products:

linked claims period.

Indicative Solution: Refers to the maximum period whereby a claimant, having been in receipt of claim payments, recovers and returns to work, but within the specified period suffers a recurrence of the same disability, and is eligible for immediate claim payments without the imposition of another deferred period.
The most popular linked claims period is six months.
The linked claims period increases the likelihood of a recovering claimant to accept to return to work without fear of what will happen if the incapacitation returns.
Both the rehabilitation benefits and linked claims period reduce the amount of claims paid to claimants.

ii.question: Set out the approach and process, including methodology and assumptions, which Company B could take to calculate the claims in payment reserves for its IP products.

Indicative Solution: Definition: The claims in payment reserves will be calculated as the expected net present value of the future claim payments and related expenses for a claim that is already being settled.
Data: Detailed data will be gathered on all active IP claims for the insurer.

Some of the key data fields that will be required include: date of disability, benefit amounts, claims duration to date, diagnosis and medical information, rehabilitation plans and prospects of returning to work.
External data may be required on recovery rates of particular disabilities, medical inflation and expenses for certain rehabilitation services.
For products with bigger claim cohorts, the data will be segmented into various cohorts that behave the same for more accurate analysis.

The segmentation may be done along the lines of diagnosis; similar medical conditions may be expected to have the same medical outlook. e.g. musculoskeletal disorders, mental health conditions, cancer.
Other segmentation options include claims duration in force, age and occupations.
However, care would be required to ensure that the segmented claims data remains credible to build assumptions from.
Broad methodology: For each claim, the company will involve experts to determine the chances of recovery, the claims and expenses that will be paid.
The experts can include inhouse claim assessors, rehabilitation nurses and the external doctors attending to the particular claimants.
Frequent and standard claims would be estimated using statistical analysis and cashflow projections in relevant cohorts accordingly.
Therefore, the actuarial team can develop reserving models to estimate the expected net present value of the future cashflows for the claims.
This will start with developing appropriate assumptions for the models.
Assumptions: The assumptions are likely to be best estimate assumptions reflecting the expected experience till the cohort of claims runs off.
Claim termination rates will be the key assumption for claims in payments and will consist of recovery to return to work or termination due to death or end of claim payment term.
The company can rely on the historical claims data to determine the recovery rates and adjust to reflect future medical trends that can affect ability to return to work.
The economic outlook may reduce the recovery rates as claimants will be worried about being redundant after going to work.
Consideration will also be made to whether the products have rehabilitation benefits and linked claims periods as these may improve the recovery rates.
Mortality rates can also be built from the historical claims data and adjusted to reflect future mortality improvements which can be provided by reinsurers or consultants.
Discount rate will be required to reflect the expected rate of return for the assets backing the reserves.
The discount rate would require to be dynamic and be consistent with the other assumptions especially inflation rates and economic performance.
Calculations: The model should project cashflows till the end of the contract boundary based on the product specifications.
Some of the cashflows may require stochastic modelling as they may be variable in themselves.
The cashflows can also be calculated per policy or per each cohort that behaves the same.
Key cashflows include the claim payments made to the claimant,
… expenses incurred whilst processing the claim payments,
… any rehabilitation expenses incurred to facilitate a faster recovery.
The expected cashflows will be discounted back to the date of valuation to determine the expected net present value.
Other considerations: The reserves calculations must follow any regulatory requirements including level of prudency prescribed by the regulator.
Sensitivity analysis must be carried out to determine how the reserves are responding to change of assumptions and assess reasonability of the sensitivities.

iii_a.question:
Company B's management is concerned that the company may be exposed to significant counterparty risks.

Discuss the key risks to which Company B may be exposed in respect of:

financial intermediaries (brokers).

Indicative Solution: The intermediaries may fail to remit premiums collected from clients on time and in extreme cases the intermediary may collapse whilst holding the insurer's funds
The brokers may mis-sell products to customers through misrepresenting the IP product or providing unsuitable advice, leading to claims and reputational damage.
They may also engage in fraudulent practices including submitting fraudulent claims in cahoots with customers.
The brokers may not encourage persistency of products if the commission structure doesn't punish high lapse rates.
Reputational missteps for the brokers may also have contagion effect on the insurer especially if the broker was the lead distribution channel for the insurer.
Brokers may lack adequate knowledge of IP insurance and relevant regulations, leading to poor advice or improper sales practices.
If the brokers face operational disruptions or challenges, they will also affect the service of clients and remitting of premiums on time to the insurer.
Brokers may mishandle client data, leading to privacy breaches and regulatory fines.

iii_b.question:
Company B's management is concerned that the company may be exposed to significant counterparty risks.

Discuss the key risks to which Company B may be exposed in respect of:

third party administration providers.

Indicative Solution: TPAs fail to meet the agreed service standards with Company B, meaning Company B fails to meet its service standards for customers.
TPA gets into financial difficulties requiring Company B to bring admin back in house at short notice.
TPAs may fail to administer claims efficiently, leading to delays and errors.
Inadequate data security measures at TPA level may lead to data leakage from the TPA side and expose customer data.
Fines and reputational damage will also affect the insurer both in terms of cost and regulatory oversight.
If the TPAs provide inconsistent or subpar customer service, this may lead to poor customer satisfaction and potentially leading to increased complaints and claims.
The TPA's technology platforms and processes may not have enough scalability which can lead to processing backlogs and delays if business traffic increases.
The TPAs may not adequately invest in their technology platforms leading to outdated platforms and compromise customer experience and faster claims payment.
If the TPAs also provide services to other insurers, the TPA may suffer from conflict of interest and not prioritise the insurer's interests.

iii_c.question:
Company B's management is concerned that the company may be exposed to significant counterparty risks.

Discuss the key risks to which Company B may be exposed in respect of:

reinsurers.

Indicative Solution: The reinsurers may fail to meet their obligations to Company B in the event of large claims or large numbers of claims.
This can cause serious liquidity challenges for the insurer as they would have settled the claims upfront. In some cases, it may cause insolvency for the insurer.
Significant contractual disputes may arise due to different interpretations of reinsurance clauses in treaty documents and facultative slips.
Resolution of such disputes through arbitration or court proceedings will lead to incurring legal expenses.
Significant changes in risk appetite for the reinsurer can increase the reinsurance premiums and reduce profits for the insurer.
The reinsurer may also reduce the cover that they are willing to provide leaving the insurer exposed to more risk and higher capital requirements.
Reinsurers may lack sufficient expertise in IP insurance, leading to inadequate pricing or risk assessment.
In the event of a widespread economic downturn or a pandemic, reinsurers may face significant losses, impacting their ability to pay claims.
If Company B cedes too much business to a single reinsurer then company B is too over exposed to default from that reinsurer.

iii_d.question:
Company B's management is concerned that the company may be exposed to significant counterparty risks.

Discuss the key risks to which Company B may be exposed in respect of:

professional advisers.

Indicative Solution: The advisers may provide negligent advice which can lead to financial losses and compliance issues for the insurer.
Without due consideration, the company may depend on external professional advisers not considering that inside expertise may have a more intimate understanding of the business.
Professional advisers may not have sufficient expertise, knowledge, or understanding of Company B's business to carry out what Company B has asked of them.
It is possible that the advisers may not declare their conflict of interest leading them to provide biased advice to the detriment of the client.
The advisers may not have scale and capacity thus leading to missing of deadlines or provision of underprepared advice and results.
Advisers may fail to communicate effectively or provide timely reports, hindering the company's decision-making.
The advisers may not follow the professional standards expected of them leading them to provide wrong advise or committing errors and omissions.

iv.question: Suggest measures that the company could adopt in order to mitigate and manage each of the risks identified in part (iii).

Indicative Solution: Financial Intermediaries (brokers): Consult the regulator on the reputable financial intermediaries that the company can use.
Put in place a service level agreement on when premiums should be remitted to the insurer and ensure that the standards are adhered to.
Implement a commission structure that encourages renewals and persistency of the business brought by brokers.
Implement a commission clawback structure that enables the company to recover commission in the event of broker misuse (e.g. churning of policies).
Analyse brokers' financial statements, credit ratings, and industry reports to assess financial stability and solvency.
Routinely investigate the brokers' reputation, ethical standards, and any history of misconduct or litigation.
Build good working relationships between the management of the company and the one for brokers.
Regularly meet with the brokers in order to provide feedback on the performance of the broker and areas of improvement going forward.
Third Party Administration (TPA) providers: Clearly communicate the company's deadlines and expectations for delivery.
Service level agreements should be made to be clear and easy to follow, monitor and enforce by the company.
Encourage customers to notify the insurer if they encounter any bad service from the TPAs. This can be done through a toll-free number.
Require that the TPAs demonstrate a good track record and have experience with serving insurers which have the same size or bigger compared to the company.
Review the company's technology platforms and processes to determine whether they can sustainably deliver the mandate that the TPA is expected to deliver.
The company can require that the TPA undergoes routine data security and penetration assessments to assure themselves that the data security measures are up to the standard required.
The TPAs may also be required to declare any conflict of interest that they may have before being onboarded by the company.
Establish contingency plans to address potential counterparty failures. This can include identifying alternative providers, securing backup systems, and establishing communication protocols.
Reinsurers: Company B could set limits on the max amount of business ceded to individual reinsurers, where the maximum amount ceded could vary depending on the credit rating of the reinsurer.
The company can adopt a policy to only work with reinsurers with a high credit rating in order to reduce the chances of reinsurer default.

The company can ensure that reinsurance contracts involve a cut-through-clause that enables the insurer to claim a portion of the reinsurance recoveries from retrocessionnaires (reinsurers of the reinsurance companies).
The company can enhance due diligence at the point of onboarding reinsurers including checking the strength of the reinsurers balance sheet and track record.
The company can involve reinsurance brokers as part of its selection process and get advice on the reputable reinsurers it can use.
Reinsurance agreements should be concise and clear so as to minimize the chances of reinsurance disputes with reinsurers.
The company's legal team and reinsurance brokers can be involved in reviewing the reinsurance agreements accordingly.
The company can use a multiple of reinsurers limiting the exposure to one reinsurer. This will spread the risk and avoid significant impact if one reinsurer defaults.
Assess the technical expertise of reinsurers to ensure that the reinsurer does have the expected expertise that the company may need in the future.
Regularly review the performance of the reinsurers during the course of treaty term or policy terms in order to identify early signs of stress.
Professional (e.g. Actuarial, Accounting and Tax) advisers: Where appropriate, require counterparties to provide collateral, guarantees, or insurance to secure their obligations.
Ensure professional advisors provide evidence of their previous work experience / knowledge / skills before being asked to take on a particular task.
Clearly define and communicate the deadlines for deliverables to the advisers so that they know what is required of them.
Put in place contingency plans that can be triggered if the adviser doesn't perform. These can be stand by advisers who can be called upon in need.
The number of advisers can also be increased in order to reduce dependency on a limited number of advisers.
The company should also consider working with other industry players in creating a record of advisers who would have provided sub-par performance so that the company doesn't onboard such advisers.
Secure appropriate insurance coverage, such as professional indemnity insurance, errors and omissions insurance, and credit insurance, to mitigate potential losses from counterparty failures.

The company can also require reference checks from industry bodies that represent the professions of advisers. For example, checking the credentials and membership status of the lead personnel for the advisers.

3.label: Question 3
3.scenario: Company C is a large health and care insurance company that sells Long Term Care Insurance (LTCI) in Country X. The company sells both pre-funded and immediate needs versions of LTCI.

i.question: Describe briefly the differences between the main product features of pre-funded LTCI and immediate needs LTCI.

Indicative Solution: Pre-funded LTCI is bought significantly prior to claim incidence hence the name pre-funded.
Immediate needs LTCI immediate commences payment of the claims soon after onboarding.
Under the immediate needs LTCI, the customer already has sub-standard health status and is concerned about their survival duration.
On the other hand, under the pre-funded policies, the customer is in good health but concerned about future survival if they meet specified definitions of illness or disability.
The premium payment under an immediate needs policy is strictly single premium.

And the prefunded LTCI has various payment options that the customer can consider. These payment options include: Single payments. Regular payments. Restricted regular payments that either stop: at a certain age; or during a defined level of disability (i.e. with a waiver of premium). Retrospective payment, from the equity released after the sale of the home.
The pre-funded product has a deferred period which differs from product to product.
Immediate needs products do not have a deferred period due to immediacy of need.
Regular premium pre-funded LTCI policies normally don't have a death benefit.
But on an immediate needs product, the policyholder can also select a death benefit.
This could be structured as a minimum payment period, or by amortising the single premium, or by providing capital protection on part of the single premium.

Basic single premium products for the pre-funded LTCI may also amortise the single premium over five years, so that on early death a part of the single premium is returned reducing from the full single premium at outset to zero after 60 months.

ii.question:
The results of Company C's recent experience investigation show that over the last 5 years the pre-funded LTCI product sold lower levels of new business than the immediate needs LTCI product.

Suggest possible reasons for this experience.

Indicative Solution: Customers prefer to buy cover once their health is failing instead of purchasing a pre-funded policy which they may not claim for in the future.
Customers could be hesitant to buy prefunded LTCI because it could be irrevocable and their circumstances may change in the future.
Customers may lack the knowledge and awareness of LTCI to take out pre-funded LTCI.
Customers could be put off by the complexity and upfront costs of taking out pre-funded LTCI.
Customers may be concerned about what happens if they die prior to LTCI benefits being paid
Customers may be unwilling to consider their need for LTCI far in advance of needing those benefits.
Customers may have other demands on their finances well in advance of needing LTCI so could be unwilling to use prefunded LTCI.
Depending on societal norms, there may be belief that the elderly will be taken care of by their younger children or relatives at old age hence the reluctance to pay for pre-funded plans.
The government may have put more supportive tax measures for the immediate needs policies compared to the pre-funded.
The pre-funded plans may have been badly designed by the company and do not meet customer needs and have low demand.
This could include a narrower or stricter list of claim definitions that make it impossible to qualify for the claim.
The company's prefunded LTCI is expensive and not competitive in the market.
It could also be that the company's immediate needs LTCI is deemed affordable and one of the most affordable in the market.
This could be further worsened by the presence of a competitor who has more credibility and market share in the pre-funded marketplace.
The pre-funded market may have been saturated hence incremental new business has significantly reduced over the years.
The immediate needs market has fewer competitors hence the company has been able to gain market share in the immediate needs market.
Customers may prefer to use the standard pension arrangement as part of retirement savings and then use the pension payouts to buy an immediate needs policy when their health becomes sub-standard.
Employers may not be paying pre-funded LTCI for employees but encourage employees to purchase immediate needs policies if they are retired on medical grounds.
The company's underwriting for the pre-funded product is very strict hence discourages customers from completing the application processes.
The company's distribution channels may be experiencing challenges explaining how the pre-funded plans operate and have developed a preference for selling immediate needs products.
The care homes in the country actively advertise their services and mention the immediate needs products available for potential customers, hence creating demand for the insurer.
Insufficient advertising of the pre-funded product by the company can also lead to less new business than expected.
The company may have become known for declining some of the claims lodged under the pre-funded product leading to bad reputation for the pre-funded product version.
The company may be offering less commissions to distribution channels on the pre-funded product compared to the immediate needs product.
It may also be possible that competition offers generous commissions on the pre-funded product hence the independent distribution channels offer the pre-funded customers to competition.
There may have been cases of mis-selling involving the pre-funded product.

Difficult economic conditions mean that customers can't afford the pre-funded product so as to keep as much disposable income. However, once their health isn't in good condition, they then buy the immediate needs policy.
The company has better reinsurance terms on the immediate needs product hence they are relatively more affordable customers compared to the pre-funded products.
The pre-funded products have high capital requirements which were affecting profitability of the company hence the company was deliberating reducing advertising of the pre-funded option.

The two products may appeal to different target markets and the target market of immediate needs products has been experiencing significant growth thus resulting in better sales compared to pre-funded products.

iii.question:
In a recent budget statement, the Minister of Finance of Country X highlighted the following expectations for the government's 5-year outlook for Country X:

• Annual gross domestic product growth is expected to be significantly lower over the next 5 years compared to the last 5 years.
• Annual inflation is expected to be significantly higher over the next 5 years compared to the last 5 years.
• The government expects to reduce direct spending on social services for the elderly and instead, promote private financing of health and care insurance for the elderly.
• The average life expectancy is expected to increase over the next 5 years from 75 years to at least 80 years.
• The government will investigate increasing the state pension age for citizens of Country X.

Discuss the opportunities and risks to Company C of each of the above expectations.

Indicative Solution: Reduced gross domestic product growth rate: A low gross domestic product growth rate will mean earnings for the customers are under pressure and customers reduce discretionary spending.
Lower customer earnings could discourage customers from taking out prefunded LTCI.
For pre-funded products, the customers may cancel the policies which would increase the lapse rates for the company.
However, the impact may be mitigated by the fact that normally do not pay surrender benefits hence no liquidity outflow may arise from the increase in lapse rates.

In addition, customers may rather prefer paid-up options hence whilst premium income may reduce, the client remains part of the customer pool and once the economy recovers, new products can be provided to the same customer.
The lapse rate impact will be minimal or non-existent for immediate needs products given the immediate needs products don't have cancellation options.
There is also an opportunity to provide options where customers can temporarily stop making payments and resume later when their economic situation has recovered.
If other competitors are not offering the same option, the offer can become a unique selling point and help the company to retain customers.
The expected period of low gross domestic product growth rate is five years which can be materially long without receiving cashflows from customers.
The low gross domestic product rate will result in returns from investments reducing which reduces investment income and therefore profitability of the company.
Reserves may therefore increase if discount rates are supposed to be reduced to reflect the low investment returns in the market.
However, this could provide an opportunity for the company to also invest offshore and diversify their asset portfolio.

If the regulator/government has previously been uncomfortable to allow insurers to invest elsewhere, the low growth environment provides opportunity for the insurers to engage government/regulator and get dispensation to invest offshore.
High inflation rate: High inflation rate erodes the disposable incomes of current and potential customers.
Hence, they may no longer afford the premiums which will lead to increase in lapse rates and slow-down in new business sales.
There is risk that the benefits that current claimants receive are eroded by inflation such that customer needs are no longer met.
Potential new customers may be discouraged if they see current claimants struggling despite having the LTCI policies. This could then result in lower new business volumes.
There is also reputational risk as media and customers complain that benefits are no longer relevant and meaningful and blaming the insurer for the challenges.
This could be mitigated if products were already indexed to inflation indices or a fixed index.
If the company did not have LTCI products that are indexed to inflation indices, the company must exploit the opportunity to launch such product variants so that the products remain relevant.
If competition does not have such indexed benefits and premiums, the company can further realise first mover advantage in the market.

Some of the LTCI products may be indemnity based which means that the company is exposed to the risk of increased medical inflation arising from care homes thus reducing profit margins for the company.
There is also the risk that expense inflation may increase at a faster pace compared to increase in premium collections. Therefore, expense margins may significantly reduce or result in losses.
The company may face loss of critical skills as employees feel that salaries are no longer meaningful and migrate to other countries.
This can lead to operational challenges due to disruption within teams.
Customer service may also deteriorate as new employees need to be trained accordingly.

Reduction of direct spending on social services and promotion of private financing of health and care insurance for the elderly: The government approach will ensure that customers who would have relied on government facilities at old age now need to make private arrangements.
The company has an opportunity to design and sell LTCI products that cover the various social services that the government no longer funds.
There is an opportunity to engage employers to consider pre-funded LTCI products for their employees so that at old age they have adequate care and decent lifestyle.

The expected difficult environment may not fully enable business development, but the company could deliberately target customers who are in the middle class and high net worth clients who may have better resilience to the inflation and low economic growth.
The company can therefore increase advertisement and promotions detailing how its products meet the needs of customers after reduced government support.
This will enable the company to increase sales and grow its premium income.

For current customers, it is also possible that with reduced government support, customers who would have lapsed their policies citing financial difficulties would reconsider and seek to continue with the policies. Hence the company should exploit the opportunity to consistently remind customers of the benefits of the LTCI policies in light of the changes in government policy.

The company also has an opportunity to influence government policy by making formal submissions to government on how the government can support private financing of long-term care needs. This would ensure that the strategies employed by government are fit for purpose and create equitable progress for both insurers, government and citizens.
Increase in life expectancy: The company may have priced the current claiming LTCI policies on lower life expectancy.
The increase in life expectancy will result in claimants living longer than expected hence more claims paid out at a loss to the company.

The same longevity risk may apply to current policies who are yet to claim. If the premium rates were guaranteed, the company will pay more claims than anticipated in the future once the policies reach claim stages.
The risk may be mitigated for policies that are reviewable hence the company can review and adjust the premiums accordingly.
However, increasing premiums may result in policy cancellations as the economy is not performing well and customers may be having reduced disposable incomes.

In addition, there is additional reputational risk if customers go on various media channels complaining about the premium reviews and portraying the company as insensitive to the economic challenges that customers are facing.
The company has an opportunity to take advantage of the increasing life expectancy to advertise about the challenges of old-age life in a higher life expectancy environment,
…..in particular increased longevity potentially increases customers chances of needing long term care.
This will encourage potential customers to buy policies and have adequate provision thus increasing new business volumes for the company.

As life expectancy increases, the number of people in need of care homes will increase. There is a risk that the available care homes are lower than the demand hence increase in care home costs and pricing. This will increase the claims for the company for cases where the company pays variable or indemnity-based payments to care homes.
Overcrowded care homes also present reputational risk for the company as the public may conclude that the company puts the elderly in overcrowded care homes.

Increase in state pension age: The increase in state pension age will mean that payment of benefits for pre-funded LTCI may be deferred further as meeting of claim definitions normally occurs after retirement.
For pre-funded LTCI, the premium payment period may further increase hence premiums are spread over more years which increases affordability for customers.
This would support growth in new business volumes for the company.

If the normal retirement is increased significantly, it may be that customers overwork for years they should have already retired. This will increase the claims incidences in the future once they have retired.
On the other hand, increase in state pension age reduces life expectancy after retirement which may reduce the length of claim payments for the company.

iv.question: Suggest possible actions that Company C could take to mitigate the risks identified in part (iii).

Indicative Solution: Customers who are not able to meet premium obligations may be provided with premium holidays so that they can resume payment when their financial situation improves.
To retain customers on the pre-funded products, customers may be offered cover on a reduced cover for a lower premium.
The company can also reduce margins in its products so that the products can be more affordable and still attract meaningful business volumes.
If the company was selling guaranteed products only, it should revert to reviewable products which can be cheaper and more affordable to customers.
The company can ensure adequate provision of information at policy application so that customers understand the limitations and risks of the product that they want to purchase.
Advertising and promotions can be carried out to ensure that customers are reminded of the benefits of LTCI cover and reduce level of lapses.
The company should also consider rationalising its operations so that it can reduce expenses and be able to sustain the premium levels that it currently has.
Potential expense reductions may include the following: Reducing staff count
Automating processes and reducing use of paper which can be high costs
Negotiating lower levels of commission with distribution channels
Providing digital channels for customers
Premiums and benefits can be indexed to inflation indexes so that levels of cover remain adequate, and premiums also grow in line with inflation.
The company can consider increasing reinsurance against longevity risk so that as lives live longer, the company is protected against significant increases in claims.
The company can increase the proportion of offshore investments to mitigate against the impact of low investment returns due to low GDP growth rates
The company can invest in assets whose returns generally track inflation including equities and properties to ensure that balance sheet erosion is minimal.
Provide rewards for customers who continue making payments so that they are motivated to keep on making payments.

For high value customers, conduct exit-interviews / surveys / feedback for cancelled policies so as to understand causes of cancellation. Some clients can be convinced not to cancel during the exit meeting.
Provide superior after-sales service to ensure customers are happy about the product and the company.
The company can consider opening operations in other surrounding countries who have a much more stable economic environment so that it diversifies its exposure.
The company can also consider introducing products that benefit from or are neutral to higher longevity risk. This will help to diversify its earnings and build more resilience for the company.
The company may consider cushioning employees from inflation through providing tangible benefits instead of monetary benefits.
The company can increase remuneration (including compensation linked to inflation) for the most critical skills that have high chances of migration to other stable countries.
Consistent training of employees and measurement of service standards will help to ensure customer service does not deteriorate.
Business continuity plans must capture key response plans if the company losses a critical resource.
For certain expenses, outsourcing operations to other companies at agreed pricing rates may help reduce the expense inflation risk for the company.
The company can consider running its own care homes through a special purpose vehicle, so that it isn't overcharged by care homes.
The company can also encourage claimants to receive care from their own homes through incentives in cases where policyholders opt to receive care from their own home.

The company may also build a preferred provider network for the care homes used by claimants so that it can ensure the care standards are above expectation and do not cause significant reputational risks.

4.label: Question 4
4.scenario:
Company D is a large health and care insurance company in Country Y, specialising in Private Medical Insurance (PMI) products. Company D's marketing director has proposed that a new PMI product is designed specifically to meet the needs of Country Y's athletes across field, track and long-distance disciplines. The marketing director's proposal is based on the following observations:

• Country Y's recent success in winning gold medals by local athletes in international events including the Olympics and the World Championships has increased the number of citizens aspiring to pursue a career in athletics.
• Country Y's existing PMI products are not tailored for the specific needs of athletes because of the higher level of injuries requiring specialist treatment and the need for medical cover at overseas events where products have territorial limits.
• Athletes are role models in society and can be used by Company D for marketing purposes to enhance its public image.

i.question: Discuss the factors that Company D should consider before agreeing to the marketing director's proposal.

Indicative Solution: Customer Needs: Athletes have special medical needs compared to the rest of the population, as they are likely to have more physical injuries than the general population.
Hence, the product should have augmented specialist treatments and rehabilitation benefits compared to standard PMI products.
However, this may increase costs of the product due to the specialist skills required to provide the health services.
Some of the athletes are international athletes requiring medical services as they participate across the global athletics calendar.
Hence, the product would need to be able to pay for medical costs incurred outside the country of origin.
Building the right network of global service providers is therefore critical for the product to meet the needs of the customers.
However, this may introduce significant currency risk of settling claims from across the world.

The company should therefore consider building a focus group made of athletes and service providers who can assist in ensuring that the product meets the needs of athletes and their medical support staff.
Simplicity and Clarity: Medical conditions for athletes are technical and specialist in nature. Hence, the definitions of the cover provided are critical.
The company can consult sports doctors and other specialists on internationally accepted definitions that can be used in the product specifications.
It may be useful to analyse products of similar nature that have been sold in other markets and consult on how they balanced the specialist nature of sports injuries and simplification.
The company will also need to ensure that the proposed definitions aren't only understood by specialist service providers but the athletes themselves have a fair understanding of the product.
Discussions with the suggested focus group can help measure their understanding of the proposed terms and conditions for the product.
A key consideration will be on what are the limits and exclusions for the product. The limits may need to be generous to accommodate specialist procedures.
Exclusions may include illness or injuries due to banned substances, and extreme hobbies that lead to serious sports complications.

Customer Demand and Marketability: Whilst the need may be apparent, the company may need to consider whether the population of athletes in the country is big enough to generate sufficient pooling of risk.
The company can consult the national sports association of the country to determine the population of athletes in the country.
Consideration must be made that not all athletes will purchase the product especially international athletes who may have international sponsors that would have arranged their medical cover.
Consider what requirements there are going to be for an athlete to be able to buy this product.
Need to consider what proportion of athletes will buy the new product.
Consider what a typical policyholder would look like.
The product may also need to be tiered to accommodate athletes who may be earning less amounts of income from their sports.
This will ensure that a greater portion of athletes can afford a level of specialist medical cover.
A sample of athletes can be asked on what they can afford so that the company can design products that are marketable to the company.
Pricing Considerations: Consider what the main drivers of risk / risk factors are for athletes. e.g. age, gender, type of event (field, track and long-distance disciplines).
The company will not have readily available data on athletes since it has not provided such a product before.
The data may be accessed from the national sports associations for the various disciplines or reinsurers who have reinsured the product in other markets.
Consider whether the company will have sufficient data for each discipline as the types of athletes covered are likely to be very diverse.
The company will need to ensure that the product is consistent with its product and pricing culture and style.
The company will also need to assess whether it can be able to attain its target return on capital.
Need to consider profitability of the product as a whole,
… and whether the product is viable to sell.
Consider what product volumes would Company D need to break even.
Underwriting: Consider whether there is a self-selection risk here, where athletes that are more predisposed to injury take out the policy and athletes less predisposed don't take out the product.
The standard underwriting procedures for a PMI product will not be sufficient or applicable to the specialist product.
The company will therefore need to build new underwriting grids or procedure manuals for the product.
Consider whether there are needs for specialist training for underwriting team for this product.
Injuries for sports persons may be recurring hence the company needs to assess whether such injuries can be deemed as pre-existing conditions.
Complete exclusion of pre-existing injuries may make the product unmarketable because most athletes are likely to have recurring injuries depending on severity.
The underwriting personnel required for the product may require specialist sports doctors who may be expensive and can distort the company's remuneration structure.
Reinsurance: The standard reinsurance treaties in place for the company are unlikely to have scope to cover the product.
The company may design with the expectation that they can place the product on a facultative basis but this can be risky if they later do not find cover.
Therefore, the company can scout for reinsurers who have experience in the sports medical cover space and seek their support and expert advice as they design the product.

Claims Procedures: Some of the medical conditions for the sports persons may require emergency assistance hence the company will need to ensure emergency procedures can be done without calling back home to the claims department.
Major cities that have significant athletics events will require to have preauthorized providers to ensure ease of access and treatment for athletes.
Given the global reach of athletes, there may be need to have a 24-hour claims call centre to ensure that claims are attended on time.
Payments will also be critical and bankers may need to be involved to ensure that service providers outside the country get payments on time.
The claims assessors may also need to be specialist sports doctors with sufficient understanding of the various medical conditions.
Regulation: Regulatory requirements will need to be considered to ensure that compliance is achieved.
The product should adhere to data privacy regulations when collecting and processing personal information from athletes.
The company may need to consider the regulator's treatment of cross border payments including exchange control requirements if applicable.
Given the potential cross border payments, currency risk may be introduced and capital requirements may significantly increase.
In addition, investments may also need to be done in other currencies other than the local currency to ensure sufficient hedging.
Other considerations: The company will need to consider the appropriate distribution channels for the product.
National associations of sports disciplines can be an easy distribution channel especially if the associations are given appropriate commissions.
However, traditional distributors may not be happy if they are excluded from selling the product.

To increase product uptake, the company can consider collaborating with sponsors of the athletes so that they can pay on behalf of athletes as part of sponsorship. This will further help to increase affordability and reduce non-renewals for the product.
The company can also consider competitor actions as they decide on the proposal.

If the market does not have the product, the company will have first mover advantage, and this can be a unique value proposition for the company. The brand equity for the company may also improve and lead to more sales across its various products.
As claimed by the marketing director, the athletes can be influencers and be valuable to the company's marketing efforts.
However, if the athletes suffer some reputational damage, this will spill over to the company's reputation.

5.label: Question 5
5.scenario:
Company E is a health and care insurance company that sells a full range of health and care products in Country Z.

Country Z's current regulatory regime requires health and care insurance companies to calculate their statutory reserves using prudent assumptions (best estimate assumptions plus a margin for prudence), plus additional capital requirements using simple formula based on written premiums.

Country Z's regulatory regime is currently being changed. The proposed changes include:

• The reserves will be calculated using best estimate assumptions.
• A separate risk margin will be calculated instead of a margin for prudence.
  ◦ The risk margin will be calculated using the 'cost of capital' approach on top of the best estimate reserves.
  ◦ This involves first projecting forward the future capital that the company is required to hold in respect of these risks, where this is determined at the end of each projection period (e.g. year) during the run-off of the business.
  ◦ The projected capital is determined according to the relevant regulatory basis and is the amount required to be held in excess of the projected liabilities at each period.
  ◦ These projected capital amounts are then multiplied by a cost of capital rate.
• In addition to the best estimate reserves and risk margin, additional solvency capital requirements will be required.
  ◦ The level of solvency capital requirements will be based on either a risk measure approach such as 'value at risk' where the insurers will apply their own judgement when setting the parameters, or a standard formula approach where Country Z's regulator will prescribe the methodology and parameters to be used by the insurers.

i.question: Discuss the factors Company E should take into consideration in assessing the potential implications of Country Z's regulatory changes.

Indicative Solution: Financial implications: In general, the reserves of the company will reduce due to the removal of the margin of prudence in the reserves and using best-estimate assumptions.
The level of reduction in the reserves will also depend on the level of the margin of prudence in the statutory basis. Higher levels of prudency mean that the new reserves will be materially lower.
However, the new approach introduces a risk margin to the overall reserving and capital framework. The sum of the new reserves plus the risk margin may still be material.
The company must therefore consider the impact of the risk margin approach compared to the margin of prudence.
The impact of the risk margin approach will also depend on the overall features and characteristics of the company's policies.
Long-dated business may have more volatility hence require more capital to be held till full run-off of the policies. This will therefore lead to a higher risk margin being required to be held.

The capital requirements changes are less prescriptive in terms of offering options to use an internal approach or a standard formula approach. The company will be able to choose an approach that results in lower capital requirements.

The new approach may either enable the company to put in place capital that better reflects its risk profile but result in higher capital requirements if the risk profile is generally higher than reflected through capital requirements.
The new approach may reflect risks that may not necessarily be reflected by written premiums. These include market risk, credit risk and operational risk.
If the change in reserving methodology results in increase in reserves, the profitability of company could reduce.

If the total of the reserves and capital requirements increases, the company will have lower solvency. This may affect its credit ratings in the market and therefore ability to raise capital from the market.

Choice between Internal Model and Standard Formula: The company must assess whether the flexibility and ability to reflect company risk profile of the internal model justifies the additional effort and costs of developing the internal model.
The overall capital requirements of the internal model may not be materially different from the results of the standard formula thus leading to wastage of resources for the company.
However, if the company is large, there is merit in using an internal model as it may have its unique circumstances that cannot be reflected in the standard formula.
In the same vein, a large company will have resources to employ skills and systems to operationalise the internal model.
Internal model will however bring more governance requirements including a rigorous external audit process including detailed justification for using an internal model.
Model development, validation, implementation and review will also be required to be meeting minimum regulatory standards thus adding additional cost for the company.

The company must assess whether the standard formula deviates significantly from its own internal risk management and reporting requirements. If it deviates significantly, development of an internal model would be justified.

There is also value in benchmarking against competitors of the same size and breadth of products to check which approach have they have chosen. This will enable the company not to take its own approach that may be difficult to justify.
Data implications: The risk margin approach may be more data intensive compared to the previous margin of prudence approach.
For example, the run-off of policies needs more detailed information for every policy and sufficient tracking of cashflows till run-off of the whole book.
Both the internal model and the standard formula are likely to add more risk pillars compared to use of written premiums as the only proxy of risk.

For example, the following additional data or information may be required: For operational risk; past operational losses incurred by the company will be required (date, type of loss, cause and amount lost are some of the fields that will be required). For credit risk; the credit history of every counterparty including their credit ratings, credit history, guarantees and collateral will be required. For market risk; detailed schedules of all assets, their valuations, history of returns, date of purchase, date of maturity etc will be required in order to determine level of risk exposure.
For the new approach, data will need to be checked for completeness, accuracy and relevance given the likely higher audit assurance and impact of wrong data on the results.
The company may need to increase the data fields it captures for current in-force policies and claims going forward in order to meet the data requirements of the new regime.

The same applies to reinsurance processes. The company will need to consistently track the credit rating of the reinsurer, their claim recovery timelines and whether they have defaulted on some claims, any repudiated claims etc.
System / IT implications: The new reserving and capital requirements approach may require systems to be changed or to add new functionalities to the systems.
The changes may include: Addition of fields captured on policies and claims
Changing frequency of carrying out certain processes e.g. to manage operational risk, day-end runs for systems may be required compared to before.
Asset registers will be required to capture more detail about the asset

The new approach is likely to be data intensive hence increase in data storage capacity may be required. Historical data may be required to be able to build parameter and assumptions thus requiring more data storage space.
Reserving models will also need to be changed to meet the new processes being required including: Addition of the risk margin approach to calculation
Addition of more risk modules in the capital requirements framework
Mapping the results to the internal financial reporting modules
Reinsurance systems may also require some changes to enable the company to capture more detail on the reinsurance of the policies, the reinsurance agreements and past performance of the reinsurer.

Pricing implications: Changes in reserving and capital frameworks will also require updates to the company's pricing models. For example, cost of capital on the risk margin will also need to be appropriately captured in the pricing.
Higher reserves and capital requirements also may increase the pricing of the product to capture the impact of cost of capital.

Due to changes in capital requirements and reserves, the company needs to assess changes in timing of cashflows and the profitability that can be achieved from its products. This will need to be captured in its pricing models.

Costs / Expenses implications: The adoption of new reserving and capital requirements will require the company to set up a project team made up of experts. This will ensure that the company doesn't assume the compliance process is business as usual and then run the risk of not meeting regulatory deadlines.
Other projects that were currently running may need to be shelved. This will create project overruns and more expenses for the other running products.
Assembling a team of experts and buying/developing the new system will increase expenses for the company including consultancy fees.
Therefore, the company must consider whether taking the standard formula approach on capital requirements will reduce the cost of implementing the project.
Controls and governance for the new regulatory regime will also increase as part of the implementation process.
Resources implications: The company may be required to recruit experts (permanently or on a temporary basis) who are competent in the new regulatory regime.

Given the market is transitioning to the new regulatory regime, all insurance companies will be trying to recruit the same skills leading to higher remuneration being required to get the skills. This may lead to higher than normal wage inflation for the company.

The company could consider upskilling the current personnel within the company or form a specific project team. However, there may then be the need to fill in the vacancies created by moving personnel to the project team.
Initially, it is important for the company to carry out a full gap analysis to determine the level of compliance that the company already meets based on the new standards.
Investment Implications: If the reserves and capital requirements of the company significantly increase, the free assets of the company may reduce significantly.
A key consideration is that the company will be forced to consider more matching of liabilities and assets in terms of nature, term and currency.
The new reserving and capital requirements may also incorporate illiquidity premium hence the asset profile may need to be adjusted accordingly.

Financial reporting and compliance implications: Internal audit processes need to be updated to incorporate the new regulatory regime so that any gaps are identified before external review by auditors.

Both internal and external audit review processes may be rigorous and demanding in the first financial year of implementing the regulatory regime. External auditors may increase their fees basing on the additional work required to assure the implementation process.
Delays in the overall financial reporting processes may therefore occur and possibly attract fines with the regulators.
Risk management: Model governance for the new regulatory regime will need to be enhanced given the new approach may have more detailed and rigorous processes.

The executive and the Board must undergo training to be able to understand their role in the implementation process and key decision points that they have to make in terms of oversight and governance.
Risk policy and appetite will be required to be adjusted to reflect the changes introduced by the regulatory regime.

Reinsurance: The company may need more support from reinsurers in terms of how to comply with the new regulatory requirements. Reinsurers with exposure to markets who have already implemented regulatory requirements that are the same as the introduced one would make a difference and assist with training.
